i see your clock program. Not too far from Beads so can't be too critical hah. I do however think that the usage of [# space] to be a comment and [## space] to be a higher level comment is understandable, but having # prefix for an identifier to mean a tracked variable seems ill-advised. Why not use one of the other dozen punctuation characters like @ or % as a significant prefix. Seems way too dangerous to overload the comment delimiter with a functional prefix, when the only difference is a small space character. Makes it much harder to read; you are trying to train the eye to skip over the comments quickly and this will impede that training. So my criticism is about ergonomics. This is one of the things i hate about languages which overload the + operator. So plus can mean in some languages vector addition, string concatenation, all sorts of stuff. In FORTH you could redefine the + operator which was insane.

i now have clock, wristwatch, tic-tac-toe, snake, chess, networked tic tac toe, and planning some for engineering use that employ lots of physical units, and then some for business that do your classic sum of territories sales report or stock portfolio.  Controlling a robot is a great idea. Sure wish Sony wasn't holding back on the Aibo; there isn't a commercial robot that is nimble that is open source yet to my knowledge. Huge mistake on Sony's part not to open it up to 3rd parties fully.

Hi Wouter, welcome! We chatted a bit about Lobster's `while` sometime back. I still find Lobster one of the most promising languages, just don't have time to explore or work with it :smile:. One really interesting feature is the typing (is there a name for this? verified duck typing? flow based?) The ability to write fairly flexible code with only a  few annotations, and the compiler will verify (whole program analysis?) that there is no AttributeError. Specifically each branch is specialized for invocation with different types. It appears you end up with Python like semantics and code patterns, except AttributeErrors are checked at compile time? Even the null checks are very nice because if something can be null or a number, you can just do
```
if a:
  a += 1
```
And the compiler wont complain. No 'unpacking sum type' is necessary, and no danger of accidentally doing `a+=1` in an unchecked branch (compiler will complain). This is kind of a sweet spot between the current mainstream static and dynamic typing ideas. Is this a correct understanding of the features?

Whare areas of Lobster are under active development?

(For folks interested in the type system see <https://htmlpreview.github.io/?https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aardappel/lobster/master/lobster/docs/type_checker.html>)

Also I really like that control structures are just functions. This is a more controversial idea, I believe, because folks argue we should only have a small set of well known control flows. But I think this is really powerful and also keeps the language core really small.
Yup, you have a pretty good picture of the type system. The goal is Python like comfort and productivity with a C# or even Rust-like safety net :slightly_smiling_face:

The magic in the type system is that it does flow sensitive specialization, so the type checking is relative to the compile-time call-graph, as if it evaluates each possible path for every type combination

One area that changed recently (and I yet have to document) is memory management: I implemented a lifetime/borrow checker similar to Rust, but unlike Rust it is mostly transparent to the programmer. In the base case it results in compile-time reference counting. Also it gained "zero-cost abstraction" in the sense that it has inline structs now
Also the beginnings of concurrency, in fully distributed, coordinated tuple space fashion
Sounds awesome. Modules would be nice too :smile:.

no plans for modules yet.. I guess I've never made anything big enough where "include" didn't work for me.

also the compilation model is "closed world", meaning it wants to see the whole program to be able to do typechecking etc. That makes it extra powerful. Usually the use of modules is couple with seperate compilation, which would require a lot of explicit typing at the boundary in the case of Lobster

Edward: thanks! Not sure about the commercial potential, it has quite a following, but not big enough to the point where if I would charge money it would make me a meaningful amount. For comparison, it received about 2k or so in donations in 10 years, which is.. less than a dollar a day? :slightly_smiling_face:
Oh I meant proper namespaces rather than compiled modules. I understand it would still need whole program analysis.

Hmm, this "Lobster" language sounds very nice, but I think distributed development - ability to combine independent components made by various groups/people - is really important. Run-time plug-in loading must be possible and ought not be difficult. That may be why I generally ignore the design space that involves whole-program analysis.

Am also a fan of runtime code generation which doesn't seem like it would mesh well with whole-program analysis. What work do you do in relation to WebAssembly? Do I understand correctly that WebAssembly doesn't support runtime codegen very well? I mean, sure you can create a module and compile it, but can the new module share the same memory as the old module? Can modules store function pointers to "any module" and call those pointers efficiently? Can the new module be garbage-collected?

I'm surprised Restructor would make a function a(C)[A] = A - C which seems entirely redundant and reduces readability... what's with the square brackets? (oh, a captured variable, I'd assumed it was some kind of param.)

David: I agree that is needed for larger teams / programs.. Lobster evolved as a nice language for 1 dude to write a game in, and it shows.. should not be hard to retrofit stricter modularity, it just means giving up on the nice type inference at the boundary.

Lobster currently supports runtime code generation, but it will simply run a second VM for that (a Lobster "VM" is pretty cheap), so that has the disadvantage that the two can only share data that was explicitly serialized.. again that can be improved.
(It uses the same trick for multi-threading)
I work on the LLVM backend for WebAssembly, but also do other things.. generally looking into supporting as many languages as possible on the platform.

wasm internally to itself certainly doesn't support runtime codegen, as you have no access to the code at all (part of its security model), but you can generate a full wasm module, hand that back to the host (e.g. JS) to spin up a second wasm module. The future will give dynamic linking to make communication with such modules easier. Kind of like generating a DLL/.so at runtime, just easier
The memory sharing will be possible in the future. yes, but not currently (likely also with the dynamic loading)
Garbage collection also coming independently :slightly_smiling_face:
Restructor in its current form goes for maximum refactoring, and does not care about readability.. yes in a practical application you'd probably want some heuristics that bound the size of functions
You know how to ask a lot of questions :stuck_out_tongue:

Yes, and actually I'd like to chat 1-on-1 because ... well, a selfish reason :slightly_smiling_face: I tried to get involved with WebAssembly but it didn't work out too well because I didn't have any meaningful connections with anybody. So, on the off chance it isn't too late, I am hoping you can help me figure out if it is possible to try again.
Sure, I don't mind.. DM me :slightly_smiling_face:
